examples.title: Process Creation: Fork-Exec-Wait Pattern
examples.description: Complete example demonstrating fork(), execl(), and waitpid() to create and manage a child process
examples.explanation: This code demonstrates the classic Unix pattern for process management. fork() creates a child process that runs in parallel with parent. The child uses execl() to replace its program with 'ls -l .'. The parent uses waitpid() to block until child completes, then retrieves exit status.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n) where n is the time to execute child process
examples.spaceComplexity: O(k) where k is the size of the child process image (copy-on-write)

examples.title: Inter-Process Communication via Pipe
examples.description: Demonstrates creating a pipe and using it for one-way communication between parent and child processes
examples.explanation: This code creates a unidirectional pipe for IPC. The parent creates the pipe before fork, so both processes inherit file descriptors. Child closes read end and writes message; parent closes write end and reads message. Closing unused ends is crucial - it signals EOF when writer closes.
examples.timeComplexity: O(m) where m is the number of bytes transferred
examples.spaceComplexity: O(P) where P is the pipe buffer size (typically 64KB)

examples.title: I/O Multiplexing with select()
examples.description: Demonstrates using select() to monitor multiple file descriptors simultaneously for reading
examples.explanation: select() allows a process to monitor multiple file descriptors for I/O readiness. It blocks until at least one descriptor is ready or timeout expires. FD_ZERO clears the set, FD_SET adds descriptors, FD_ISSET checks which are ready. This is fundamental for servers handling multiple clients.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n) where n is number of file descriptors being monitored
